sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:

Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 253 actions; a strong score needs about 61 or fewer. You are 4.1x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the path extended RIGHT but didn't collect the blue block. The blue block is still at its position. The path seems to be at the player's row (around 23-28), but the blue block is at rows 25-28. Maybe the path didn't reach the blue block because the player's row doesn't fully overlap with the blue block's row.
Wait, actually looking more carefully at the image, the path is at the bottom of the dark area (around rows 36-41), not at the player's current position. That's strange. Let me check the current positions.
Actually, I think the player moved to rows 23-28 (after moving DOWN 5 from 18-23), but the path extended at a different row. Let me check the current positions.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current positions
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ('R', 'b', 'N', 'M'):
        boundary = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in boundary]
        cols = [p[1] for p in boundary]
        if min(rows) < 50:
            print(f"{n['color']}: rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
R: rows=19-22, cols=18-21, pixels=16
b: rows=25-28, cols=42-45, pixels=16
N: rows=31-34, cols=42-45, pixels=16
M: rows=36-41, cols=11-16, pixels=18
M: rows=38-39, cols=13-14, pixels=4

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
I see - the player is at rows 36-41, not rows 23-28. So the DOWN movement didn't work as expected, or the player somehow moved back down. Let me check what happened.
Wait, looking at the previous output, the player was at rows 18-23 before moving DOWN 5. After moving DOWN 5, the player should be at rows 23-28. But now the player is at rows 36-41. That's a big jump.
Oh wait, I think the DOWN movement was executed, but then when I extended RIGHT, something happened that moved the player back. Or maybe the player position I saw earlier was wrong.
Actually, looking at the image, the player (pink square) is at the bottom of the track, at rows 36-41. The path extended RIGHT from the player's position. The path is at the bottom of the dark area, below all three blocks.
So the player is at rows 36-41, which is below all three blocks. The path extends horizontally at rows 36-41, which doesn't overlap with any block.
Let me reconsider. The player needs to be at the same row as a block to collect it. The blocks are at:
- Red: rows 19-22
- Blue: rows 25-28
- Green: rows 31-34
The player is at rows 36-41. To collect a block, I need to move the player to the block's row.
Let me move UP to align with green (rows 31-34). The player is at rows 36-41, so I need to move UP about 3 steps to reach rows 33-38, which overlaps with green at rows 31-34 (overlap at rows 33-34).
Actually wait, I'm confused about the player's position. Let me look at the image again. The player (pink square with white center) is at the bottom of the gray track on the left. The track goes from the top to the bottom of the dark area. The player is at the bottom of this track.
Looking at the image, the player is at the bottom of the track. The path extends RIGHT from the player's position. The path is at the bottom of the dark area, below all three blocks.
So the player is at rows ~36-41. To collect green (rows 31-34), I need to move UP. To collect blue (rows 25-28), I need to move UP more. To collect red (rows 19-22), I need to move UP even more.
Let me first retract the path, then move UP to align with green, then extend RIGHT to collect green.
